items.q: When might an autocratic leadership style be appropriate?
items.correct: In crises requiring quick decisions, when workers are inexperienced, or in highly hierarchical contexts — though it can damage morale long-term.
items.misconception: Autocratic leadership is always wrong and democratic leadership is always right.
items.random_wrong: Autocratic leaders are dictators who break the law.
items.why: Students moralise leadership styles. The business studies view is contingency-based — each style has appropriate contexts. Democratic is not universally best; autocracy works in emergencies.

items.correct: The external macro-environment: Political, Economic, Social, Technological, Legal and Environmental factors affecting a business.
items.misconception: PESTLE analyses the internal strengths and weaknesses of a business.
items.random_wrong: PESTLE stands for Profit, Earnings, Sales, Trade, Loans, Equity.
items.why: Students confuse PESTLE (external) with SWOT (internal + external). PESTLE is about the wider environment a business cannot control; SWOT mixes internal and external.

items.q: How does HRM differ from traditional personnel management?
items.correct: HRM treats people as a strategic asset and integrates human resource policies with business strategy; personnel management is more administrative and reactive, handling hiring, pay and disputes.
items.misconception: HRM and personnel are exactly the same thing — just a name change.
items.random_wrong: HRM only applies to large multinational companies.
items.why: The shift from personnel to HRM is conceptual — from administrative function to strategic partner. Students treat the relabel as cosmetic, missing the strategic management literature behind it.

items.q: What does the 'S' in SMART objectives stand for?
items.why: It's Specific. “Strategic” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Which 1970 essay argued the 'social responsibility of business is to increase profits'?
items.why: It's Friedman. “Drucker” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: How many elements are in the SMART acronym?
items.why: The real figure is 5 elements. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
